tier_2_handbook.definition: Penile cancer is a rare malignancy that arises from the squamous epithelial cells of the penis, accounting for approximately 95% of penile neoplasms [28]. It predominantly presents as squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) or its precursor, penile intraepithelial neoplasia (PeIN). The primary lesion most commonly occurs on the glans (34.5% of cases in the United States), followed by the prepuce (13.2%), the shaft (5.3%), overlapping sites (4.5%), and unspecified locations (42.5%) [9]. Anatomically, the penis comprises the glans, inner preputial layer, coronal sulcus, and shaft, all of which can be involved. The embryological origin is from ectodermal and mesodermal tissues, with the squamous epithelium derived from ectoderm. Invasive SCC is characterized by penetration through the basement membrane into the lamina propria, with progressive invasion into corpus spongiosum (T2), corpora cavernosum (T3), or adjacent structures such as the scrotum, prostate, or pubic bone (T4) [29]. The clinical diagnosis requires thorough history and physical examination, histologic biopsy (punch, excisional, or incisional), and assessment of human papillomavirus (HPV) and HIV status [PN-1]. The disease has a high propensity for lymphatic spread, first to inguinal lymph nodes and subsequently to pelvic nodes; involvement of the inguinal lymph nodes is the single most important prognostic factor [32]. The 5-year survival rate is approximately 65% overall, ranging from 79% for localized disease to 10% for distant metastatic disease [5,6]. Early diagnosis is critical to avoid disfigurement and improve outcomes.

subtypes.name: HPV-associated squamous cell carcinoma
subtypes.description: Invasive SCC linked to high-risk HPV infection, particularly types 16, 6, and 18. Includes several histologic patterns. p16 immunohistochemistry is often used as a surrogate marker.
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 45–80% of penile cancers are HPV-related [4,10,12-14].
subtypes.key_associations: HPV positivity is a favorable prognostic factor for disease-specific survival [15-17]. Subtypes include basaloid, warty (condylomatous, bowenoid), clear cell, lymphoepithelioma-like, and mixed.

subtypes.name: HPV-independent squamous cell carcinoma
subtypes.description: SCC not associated with HPV infection; often arises in the setting of chronic inflammation, lichen sclerosus, or phimosis. Tends to have a worse prognosis.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for the remaining 20–55% of cases [4,10].
subtypes.key_associations: Includes usual type (with pseudohyperplastic and pseudoglandular variants), verrucous carcinoma (including carcinoma cuniculatum), papillary, sarcomatoid, and mixed subtypes [PN-F]. Verrucous carcinoma has the best prognosis and does not metastasize [PN-F].

subtypes.name: Penile intraepithelial neoplasia (PeIN)
subtypes.description: Premalignant high-grade intraepithelial lesion. Two main categories: HPV-associated (basaloid, warty, pagetoid, clear cell patterns) and differentiated (HPV-independent).
subtypes.frequency: Precursor to invasive SCC; exact population frequency not specified, but clinical entities include bowenoid papulosis, erythroplasia of Queyrat, and Bowen's disease [28].
subtypes.key_associations: High risk of progression to invasive SCC if untreated. Differentiated PeIN is HPV-independent [PN-F].

subtypes.name: Aggressive histologic subtypes
subtypes.description: Subtypes recognized as having aggressive behavior: basaloid, clear cell, and sarcomatoid SCC [PN-F].
subtypes.frequency: Less common; exact frequencies not provided.
subtypes.key_associations: Basaloid and clear cell are HPV-associated; sarcomatoid is HPV-independent. These subtypes carry a worse prognosis [30,31].

subtypes.name: Squamous cell carcinoma, not otherwise specified (NOS)
subtypes.description: Invasive keratinizing carcinoma without special features; used when p16 evaluation is not available.
subtypes.frequency: Common designation when specific subtyping is not performed.
subtypes.key_associations: NOS category is used when HPV status is unknown [PN-F].

subtypes.name: Other epithelial tumors
subtypes.description: Rare variants including adenosquamous carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma, and extramammary Paget disease [PN-F].
subtypes.key_associations: Their natural history and optimal management are not well defined; treatment follows SCC principles.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of penile SCC is not fully elucidated, but the guideline highlights two primary pathways: HPV-dependent and HPV-independent. Approximately 45–80% of penile cancers harbor HPV DNA, with a strong correlation to types 16, 6, and 18 [4,10,12-14]. HPV-mediated carcinogenesis involves E6 and E7 oncoproteins that inactivate p53 and Rb, leading to genomic instability and p16 overexpression. p16 positivity (by immunohistochemistry) is a favorable prognostic factor and is used as a surrogate for HPV status [15-17]. In HPV-independent tumors, chronic inflammation, lichen sclerosus, and phimosis are implicated, often associated with TP53 mutations (though specific gene frequencies are not provided in the guideline). Other driver mutations or signaling pathways are not enumerated in the NCCN text; however, the guideline recommends multigene panel testing (MGPT) in a CLIA-approved laboratory for patients with metastatic disease to identify rare but actionable mutations and fusions (e.g., MSI-H, dMMR, TMB-H) [PN-10]. The text notes that emerging knowledge of the molecular landscape may lead to future targeted therapies [MS-18]. No specific chromosomal abnormalities or recurrent mutations (e.g., NOTCH1, PIK3CA) are mentioned in the source, so they are not included here. The broad molecular profiling approach is intended to detect alterations that might qualify for tumor-agnostic treatments (e.g., pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H tumors) [PN-D 3]. In summary, the primary known molecular drivers are HPV oncoproteins in the majority of cases, and p16 status is the only routinely used biomarker for prognosis and subtype classification.

epidemiology.incidence: Penile SCC represents 0.4% to 0.6% of all malignant neoplasms among males in the United States and Europe [1]. In 2026, an estimated 2260 new cases of penile and other male genital cancers are expected in the United States, with 450 predicted cancer-specific deaths [2]. Incidence is higher in developing regions of Asia, Africa, and South America [3].
epidemiology.mortality: The overall 5-year survival rate is approximately 65%: 79% for localized disease, 57% for regional disease, and 10% for distant metastatic disease [5,6]. Stage IV disease (pelvic or distant metastases) carries a 5-year survival of 0–66% (mean ~10%) [6,165-169].
epidemiology.trends: Data on temporal trends are not explicitly provided in the guideline, but the disease remains rare in industrialized countries.
epidemiology.demographics: The most common age at presentation is between 50 and 70 years, with a median age at diagnosis in the United States of 68 years [4,9]. The majority of patients are males; the guideline uses inclusive language but notes most studies report data predominantly in cisgender men. Race/ethnicity-specific incidence data are not provided in the source.

risk_factors.detail: Inability to retract the foreskin; hinders inspection and hygiene. Associated with a 25–60% increase in penile cancer risk [4,10,11].
Neonatal circumcision is associated with lower risk; protective effect not seen in adults [20]. May reduce risk of invasive but not in situ cancer [21].
risk_factors.strength: Moderate; number needed to treat is high

risk_factors.detail: Smokers are 3 to 4.5 times more likely to develop penile cancer [12,22].
Overall, 45–80% of penile cancers are HPV-related; especially types 16, 6, and 18 [4,10,12-14]. HPV/p16 positivity is a favorable prognostic factor [15-17].
Increased risk of HPV acquisition and lower HPV clearance; oral HPV more common in HIV-positive males [18,19]. HIV status should be assessed [PN-1].
risk_factors.strength: Relative risk for HPV acquisition 2.64 (95% CI 2.04–3.42) [18]

risk_factors.detail: Patients have a 2–9% risk of developing penile carcinoma [23-25].
Chronic inflammatory conditions and poor hygiene are recognized risk factors [4].
risk_factors.strength: Qualitative; no specific odds ratio provided

risk_factors.detail: Patients with psoriasis undergoing PUVA have increased incidence of penile cancer (286 times the general population) [26]. Shielding during treatment is recommended.
Risk increases with age; median age at diagnosis 68 years [9].
Listed as a risk factor in the primary evaluation [PN-1].

risk_factors.factor: Visceral metastases and ECOG performance status ≥1
risk_factors.detail: Poor prognostic factors for overall survival and progression-free survival in advanced disease [27].

protective_factors: Neonatal circumcision: Associated with a lower rate of penile cancer, likely due to elimination of phimosis and reduced incidence/duration of HPV infections [20]. The protective effect is not observed in adults who undergo circumcision later in life [20]. A small study suggests circumcision may reduce the risk of invasive penile cancer but not carcinoma in situ (PeIN) [21]. The number needed to treat to prevent one case is relatively high due to disease rarity.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Penile squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) most commonly presents as a palpable, visible lesion on the penis, which may be associated with penile pain, discharge, bleeding, or a foul odor if the patient delays seeking medical treatment. The lesion may be characterized as nodular, ulcerative, or fungating, and may be obscured by phimosis. Patients may exhibit signs of more advanced disease, including palpable inguinal nodes and/or constitutional symptoms (eg, fatigue, weight loss) [MS-3]. The median age at diagnosis in the United States is 68 years, with an increase in risk for individuals >50 years [MS-3]. The most common sites are the glans (34.5% of cases), prepuce (13.2%), shaft (5.3%), overlapping (4.5%), and unspecified (42.5%) [MS-3]. Early diagnosis is critical, as the 5-year survival rate is approximately 65% overall, but drops to 57% with regional disease and 10% with distant disease [MS-2].

symptoms.name: Penile lesion
symptoms.detail: A palpable, visible growth on the penis that may be nodular, ulcerative, or fungating. Often painless initially but can become painful with ulceration or infection.
symptoms.frequency: Present in nearly all symptomatic cases
symptoms.alarm: Any new or changing penile lesion requires evaluation

symptoms.name: Penile pain or discomfort
symptoms.detail: May occur due to ulceration, secondary infection, or tumor invasion into sensitive structures.

symptoms.name: Penile discharge or bleeding
symptoms.detail: Blood or serosanguinous discharge from the lesion or urethra.

symptoms.name: Foul odor
symptoms.detail: Associated with necrotic or superinfected tumors.
symptoms.frequency: More common with advanced or neglected disease
symptoms.alarm: Indicates potential for infection and advanced disease

symptoms.name: Constitutional symptoms
symptoms.detail: Fatigue, weight loss, malaise – indicate advanced or metastatic disease.
symptoms.frequency: Uncommon at initial presentation, more frequent with distant metastases
symptoms.alarm: May signify metastatic spread, requires immediate staging

signs.name: Palpable penile mass
signs.detail: The primary tumor is usually firm, irregular, and may be fixed to underlying structures. Location can be on glans, prepuce, or shaft.

signs.name: Inguinal lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Palpable inguinal lymph nodes (unilateral or bilateral) may be mobile or fixed. Fixed nodes indicate advanced disease (cN3).
signs.frequency: Approximately 30–50% of patients with palpable nodes have metastatic disease; the rest have reactive inflammation [MS-11]

signs.name: Phimosis
signs.detail: Inability to retract the foreskin, which may obscure the underlying lesion. Patients with phimosis have a 25–60% increased risk of penile cancer [MS-3].
signs.frequency: Common, especially in populations with low circumcision rates

signs.name: Lichen sclerosus
signs.detail: Chronic inflammatory condition of the glans/prepuce, associated with a 2–9% risk of developing penile carcinoma [MS-3].
signs.frequency: Present in a subset of patients

signs.name: Edema of penis, scrotum, or legs
signs.detail: Indicates lymphatic obstruction from bulky inguinal or pelvic nodal metastases.
signs.frequency: Uncommon at presentation, suggests advanced nodal disease (N3)

atypical_or_missed: Lesions obscured by phimosis, leading to delayed diagnosis until advanced stage.
Early-stage disease may be mistaken for balanitis, condyloma acuminatum, or other benign conditions.
Verrucous carcinoma can be mistaken for a large benign wart due to its exophytic, well-differentiated appearance.
Patients with psoriasis undergoing PUVA treatment have an increased incidence (286 times) of penile cancer; any penile lesion in this population should be closely monitored [MS-3].
HIV-positive patients have higher HPV acquisition and lower clearance rates, increasing risk of penile cancer; lesions in this group may be overlooked [MS-3].

red_flags: Phimosis that prevents complete examination of the glans and prepuce – requires manual retraction or dorsal slit to evaluate.
Non-healing ulcer or persistent lesion on the penis lasting more than 4–6 weeks.
Rapidly growing penile mass, especially with fixation to underlying corpora or skin.
Palpable inguinal lymph nodes that are firm, fixed, or matted (cN3) – indicates advanced nodal disease.
Constitutional symptoms (unexplained weight loss, fatigue, fever) in the setting of a penile lesion – suggests metastatic disease.
Prior history of HPV-related anogenital disease or HIV infection – high-risk populations warrant lower threshold for biopsy.
Psoriasis patients with PUVA therapy who develop any penile lesion – requires immediate evaluation due to 286-fold increased risk [MS-3].
Lichen sclerosus with any suspicious change in penile skin – carries 2–9% risk of malignant transformation [MS-3].

diagnostic.rationale: Initial evaluation identifies risk factors (balanitis, chronic inflammation, penile trauma, lack of neonatal circumcision, tobacco use, lichen sclerosus, poor hygiene, sexually transmitted diseases including HIV). Lesion characteristics (diameter, location, number, morphology [papillary, nodular, ulcerous, or flat], relationship to other structures [submucosal, corpora spongiosa, cavernosa, urethra]) are documented [PN-1].
diagnostic.key_findings: Risk factor assessment guides suspicion. Documenting lesion size, location, and depth of invasion helps determine clinical stage and surgical approach.

diagnostic.test: Punch, excisional, or incisional biopsy of primary lesion
diagnostic.rationale: Required for histologic diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma and to determine grade, depth of invasion, lymphovascular invasion (LVI), perineural invasion (PNI), and HPV status [PN-1].
diagnostic.key_findings: Provides definitive diagnosis and distinguishes PeIN (carcinoma in situ) from invasive SCC. Identifies aggressive histologic subtypes (basaloid, clear cell, sarcomatoid) and verrucous carcinoma (low malignant potential). Grade is 3-tiered: G1 well differentiated, G2 moderately differentiated, G3 poorly differentiated/undifferentiated. Any proportion of anaplastic cells classifies as G3 [PN-F, MS-4].

diagnostic.rationale: HPV (especially types 16, 6, 18) is associated with 45–80% of penile cancers. HPV/p16 positivity is a favorable prognostic factor for disease-specific survival (DSS). Assessment can be done by morphology, p16 immunohistochemistry, or molecular methods [PN-1, PN-F, MS-3].
diagnostic.key_findings: Positivity may guide counseling, screening of sexual partners, and potential future targeted therapies. p16 IHC is a surrogate marker for HPV association.

diagnostic.rationale: HIV infection increases risk of HPV acquisition and persistence, and is associated with penile cancer risk [MS-3].
diagnostic.key_findings: Positive HIV status may affect treatment decisions and requires multidisciplinary care.

staging.rationale: Required for initial staging to evaluate for nodal and distant metastases. Modalities include CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast (preferred for nodal evaluation if difficult to assess clinically), and chest x-ray or CT chest [PN-E].
staging.indication: All patients with biopsy-proven penile cancer, especially those with ≥T1 disease or palpable nodes.

staging.rationale: Considered in patients with suspected inguinal lymph node-positive disease to assess extent of nodal and distant metastases. PET/CT has high pooled sensitivity (96.4%) for cN+ disease but low sensitivity (56.5%) for cN0 disease [PN-E, MS-9].
staging.indication: Patients with clinically node-positive (cN+) penile cancer following initial CT/MRI showing concern for more extensive disease.

staging.test: MRI with contrast of pelvis/inguinal region
staging.rationale: May be used to evaluate depth of tumor invasion and nodal involvement, especially when physical examination is limited (e.g., obesity, prior inguinal surgery) [PN-E, MS-9].
staging.indication: When physical examination of the inguinal region is difficult or to assess primary tumor invasion into corpora.

staging.rationale: Can be used at the time of clinical examination if abnormal findings, patient has obesity, or prior inguinal surgery. May be combined with fine-needle aspiration (FNA) for suspicious nodes [PN-E, MS-10].
staging.indication: Targeted evaluation of palpable nodes or surveillance in difficult-to-examine patients.

staging.test: Percutaneous lymph node biopsy (ultrasound- or CT-guided)
staging.rationale: Recommended for patients with palpable inguinal nodes to confirm metastatic involvement before proceeding with definitive therapy. FNA or core biopsy of the most accessible node (inguinal or pelvic) [PN-4, PN-5].
staging.indication: Patients with palpable inguinal lymph nodes to distinguish reactive from metastatic disease. May also be used for pelvic nodes if technically feasible [PN-7].

staging.rationale: If percutaneous biopsy is negative but clinical suspicion remains high, excisional biopsy of the most suspicious node can be performed [PN-4, PN-5].
staging.indication: Negative percutaneous biopsy with high clinical suspicion or when percutaneous approach is non-diagnostic.

biomarkers.test: HPV/p16 status (p16 immunohistochemistry or molecular methods)
biomarkers.rationale: Prognostic significance – HPV/p16 positivity is associated with better disease-specific survival. Helps classify tumors as HPV-associated or HPV-independent, which may have different natural histories [PN-F, MS-3].
biomarkers.when: Should be assessed on the diagnostic biopsy or surgical specimen for all penile SCC cases.

biomarkers.test: Multigene panel testing (MGPT) in CLIA-approved laboratory
biomarkers.rationale: Considered for patients with metastatic disease to identify rare but actionable mutations and fusions, including tumor-agnostic labels that cover penile cancer (e.g., MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H) [PN-10, PN-D3].
biomarkers.when: Recommended for patients with metastatic or recurrent disease to guide subsequent-line systemic therapy options.

biomarkers.test: Microsatellite instability (MSI) or mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR) testing
biomarkers.rationale: Identifies patients who may benefit from pembrolizumab if unresectable or metastatic and progressed on prior treatment [PN-D3].
biomarkers.when: As part of MGPT or if considering pembrolizumab in the subsequent-line setting.

biomarkers.rationale: TMB ≥10 mut/Mb qualifies for pembrolizumab in patients with progressed advanced solid tumors [PN-D3].
biomarkers.when: When subsequent-line systemic therapy is considered and prior testing is negative for MSI-H/dMMR.

do_not_miss: Histologic diagnosis via biopsy (punch, excisional, or incisional) – mandatory before treatment planning.
HPV status assessment – prognostic and may affect management.
Imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast – essential for staging nodal and distant metastases [PN-E].
Complete physical examination of the penis and inguinal regions – including assessment of lesion characteristics and node palpability.
Percutaneous biopsy of palpable inguinal nodes ≥4 cm or fixed nodes before proceeding to definitive nodal therapy.
Molecular testing (MGPT) for metastatic disease – may uncover actionable targets.
avoid: Avoid routine FDG-PET/CT for cN0 disease – low sensitivity (56.5%) for occult metastases [MS-9].
Avoid bleomycin-containing chemotherapy regimens – associated with unacceptable pulmonary toxicity [PN-D2].
Avoid immediate ILND based solely on palpable lymphadenopathy without biopsy – 30–50% of palpable nodes are reactive [MS-11].
Avoid modified inguinal lymphadenectomy that excludes central and superior zones of the inguinal region – false-negative rate of 15% with older technique [MS-11].
Avoid routine DSNB in centers without substantial experience – should be limited to centers performing at least 20 procedures per year [MS-10].

staging.system: AJCC 8th Edition (2017) TNM Staging System for Penile Cancer

t_stages.description: Noninvasive localized squamous cell carcinoma (including verrucous carcinoma which is by definition well-differentiated, see PN-3)

Glans: Tumor invades lamina propria. Foreskin: Tumor invades dermis, lamina propria, or dartos fascia. Shaft: Tumor invades connective tissue between epidermis and corpora regardless of location. All sites with or without lymphovascular invasion (LVI) or perineural invasion (PNI) and is or is not high grade (i.e., grade 3 or sarcomatoid). Subdivided into T1a (without LVI or PNI and not high grade) and T1b (exhibits LVI and/or PNI or is high grade) [ST-1].
Tumor invades lamina propria (glans), dermis/lamina propria/dartos fascia (foreskin), or connective tissue (shaft) without LVI or PNI and is not high grade (grade 1–2).
Tumor invades lamina propria (glans), dermis/lamina propria/dartos fascia (foreskin), or connective tissue (shaft) with LVI and/or PNI or is high grade (grade 3 or sarcomatoid).
Tumor invades into corpus spongiosum (either glans or ventral shaft) with or without urethral invasion.
Tumor invades into corpora cavernosum (including tunica albuginea) with or without urethral invasion.
Tumor invades into adjacent structures (i.e., scrotum, prostate, pubic bone).

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed
No palpable or visibly enlarged inguinal lymph nodes
Palpable mobile unilateral inguinal lymph node
Palpable mobile ≥2 unilateral inguinal nodes or bilateral inguinal lymph nodes
Palpable fixed inguinal nodal mass or pelvic lymphadenopathy (unilateral or bilateral)
Lymph node metastasis cannot be established
≤2 unilateral inguinal metastases, no extranodal extension (ENE)
≥3 unilateral inguinal metastases or bilateral metastases
ENE of lymph node metastases or pelvic lymph node metastases (with or without ENE)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Carcinoma in situ (PeIN); premalignant, high risk of progression to invasive SCC [MS-3].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative - organ-sparing approaches (topical therapy, excision, laser).
Curative - organ-sparing approaches (excision, laser, or topical therapy for verrucous? Note: verrucous is well-differentiated, surveillance of nodes only required [PN-3]).

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Low-risk primary tumor with no adverse features; low risk of occult nodal metastases (<17%) [MS-15].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative - organ-sparing approaches (wide local excision, glansectomy in select cases, laser, RT).

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Intermediate- to high-risk primary tumor; risk of occult nodal metastases 68–73% for T1b/T2 [MS-15]. Invasion into corpus spongiosum (T2).
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Approximately 57% (regional disease) if nodal involvement develops; if node-negative, ~79%.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative - partial/total penectomy plus inguinal lymph node dissection (ILND) or DSNB for high-risk features [PN-2, PN-3].

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Invasion into corpora cavernosum; associated with worse cancer-specific survival and higher lymph node metastasis rates compared to T2 [MS-5].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Approximately 57% (regional disease) if node-negative; worse if node-positive.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative - partial/total penectomy + ILND (or DSNB if high-risk) [PN-2, PN-3].

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: ≤2 unilateral inguinal metastases without ENE (pN1); regional disease with good prognostic features.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 57% (regional disease overall); higher with pN1 (70% range) [MS-13].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative - ILND (standard or modified) with adjuvant therapy based on risk (if pN2–3 then PLND, RT, chemo).

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: ≥3 unilateral or bilateral inguinal metastases (pN2); high-risk nodal disease.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Approximately 30–50% depending on extent of nodal disease.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative - ILND + PLND + neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy (chemotherapy, RT, or chemoradiotherapy).

stage_groups.criteria: Any of: T4, Any N, M0; Any T, N3, M0; Any T, Any N, M1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: T4 (invasion into adjacent structures); N3 (fixed inguinal mass or pelvic nodes, or ENE); M1 (distant metastases). Pelvic node metastasis is ominous with 5-year survival <10% [MS-17].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 10% for distant disease; <10% for pelvic nodal disease; <20% overall for stage IV [MS-2, MS-17].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative or curative intent in selected cases (neoadjuvant chemotherapy + consolidation surgery if responsive). Systemic therapy + RT for local control. Best supportive care for refractory disease.

staging_pearls: The distinction between T1a and T1b is based on LVI, PNI, and grade: T1b has LVI and/or PNI or is high grade (G3 or sarcomatoid) [ST-1].
T2 now only includes corpus spongiosum invasion (not cavernosum). T3 is corpora cavernosum invasion (including tunica albuginea). This is a change from the 7th edition [MS-4].

pN1 allows up to 2 unilateral inguinal metastases without ENE; pN2 is ≥3 unilateral or bilateral; pN3 includes ENE or pelvic node metastases. This subclassification better stratifies prognosis [MS-4].
cN3 includes palpable fixed inguinal mass OR pelvic lymphadenopathy (unilateral or bilateral) [ST-1].
Stage II splits into IIA (T1b/T2N0) and IIB (T3N0) recognizing different outcomes for corpus spongiosum vs cavernosum invasion [MS-4].
Verrucous carcinoma (Ta) is by definition well-differentiated; it rarely metastasizes and surveillance of inguinal nodes is sufficient [PN-3].
Imaging (CT/MRI) uses 4 cm threshold for defining bulky nodal disease; this size threshold is the maximum diameter of contiguous inguinal node tissue on physical exam or axial imaging [PN-4].

The AJCC recommends recording site-specific factors: percentage of tumor poorly differentiated, depth of invasion in verrucous carcinoma, presence of LVI/PNI, size of largest LN metastasis, total number of lymph nodes removed [MS-4].
About 20–30% of patients with positive inguinal nodes will also have pelvic node metastases, but skip metastases (pelvic without inguinal) are extremely rare [MS-12, MS-17].

management_principles.overview: Penile squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is a rare malignancy (0.4%–0.6% of male cancers in the US/Europe) with an estimated 2260 new cases and 450 deaths in 2026 [2]. A multimodal approach is essential, integrating surgical, radiation, and systemic therapies tailored to disease stage and patient fitness. Treatment philosophy emphasizes organ preservation when oncologically safe, particularly for early-stage (PeIN, Ta, pT1a) lesions, while radical surgery (partial/total penectomy) remains standard for invasive disease. Regional lymph node management is critical, as nodal status is the strongest prognostic factor [32]. The NCCN Panel relies on expert consensus due to limited prospective trials, with category 2A recommendations as default.

by_intent.approach: Penile organ-sparing approaches (topical therapy, wide local excision, laser, glansectomy, Mohs surgery) for PeIN/Ta/T1a; partial or total penectomy for T1b/T2+; bilateral inguinal lymph node dissection (ILND) or dynamic sentinel node biopsy (DSNB) for intermediate/high-risk nodal basins. Surveillance for low-risk N0.

by_intent.patient_group: Regionally advanced (≥cN1, bulky inguinal nodes, pelvic nodes)
by_intent.approach: Neoadjuvant TIP (paclitaxel, ifosfamide, cisplatin) followed by ILND ± pelvic lymph node dissection (PLND) for responders; adjuvant chemotherapy, radiotherapy (RT), or chemoradiotherapy (CRT) for high-risk features (extranodal extension, ≥2 positive nodes, pelvic involvement).

First-line systemic therapy (preferred: TIP; alternatives: cisplatin/fluorouracil, cisplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab, carboplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab). Subsequent-line options include pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H tumors (tumor-agnostic labels), paclitaxel, cetuximab, or clinical trials. RT for local control/palliation. Best supportive care per NCCN Palliative Care Guidelines.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: The NCCN Panel includes urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, pathologists, radiologists, and patient advocates. Multidisciplinary review is recommended for treatment planning, especially for complex cases involving neoadjuvant therapy, combined modality treatment, or salvage surgery.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: ECOG performance status (PS) is a relevant prognostic factor: a study of advanced penile SCC identified ECOG PS ≥1 and visceral metastases as poor prognostic factors for both overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS) [27]. Patients with ECOG 0–1 are generally candidates for intensive chemotherapy regimens (e.g., TIP). Those with ECOG ≥2 may require dose modifications or non-cisplatin-based alternatives; cisplatin eligibility should be assessed (renal function, hearing, neuropathy). For patients ineligible for neoadjuvant TIP, surgery without neoadjuvant chemotherapy is recommended [2].

management_pathways.title: Primary Treatment for PeIN or Ta

nodes.criteria: Penile intraepithelial neoplasia (PeIN) or Ta (noninvasive verrucous carcinoma)

options.label: Topical therapy
options.recommendation: Imiquimod 5% cream apply at night three times per week for 4–16 weeks; or Fluorouracil cream 5% apply twice daily for 2–6 weeks.

options.label: Wide local excision
options.recommendation: Complete excision with negative margins; circumcision alone for distal prepuce tumors; STSG or FTSG if needed.

options.label: Laser therapy
options.recommendation: CO2, Nd:YAG, or KTP laser; perioperative acetic acid application to identify HPV-infected skin; plume evacuator required.

options.label: Complete glansectomy
options.recommendation: For select distal tumors; negative frozen sections of cavernosal bed and urethral stump; STSG/FTSG for neoglans.

options.label: Mohs micrographic surgery
options.recommendation: Alternative to wide local excision in select cases; serial excision with microscopic evaluation; best for small superficial proximal lesions.

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy indicated after complete excision. Surveillance per PN-8.

nodes.criteria: pT1a (no LVI, no PNI, not high grade)

options.label: Wide local excision
options.recommendation: Acceptable organ-sparing option with close follow-up.

options.label: Partial penectomy
options.recommendation: Standard if organ-sparing not feasible; intraoperative frozen sections for negative margins.

options.label: Glansectomy in select cases
options.recommendation: Only if required to ensure complete tumor eradication with negative margins.

options.label: Mohs surgery
options.recommendation: In select cases.

options.label: Laser therapy
options.recommendation: Acceptable outcomes.

options.label: Radiation therapy (RT)
options.recommendation: Brachytherapy with interstitial implant or EBRT 65–70 Gy with conventional fractionation, with bolus and 2 cm margins. Circumcision should precede RT.

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy recommended if negative margins. Surveillance per PN-8.

nodes.criteria: pT1b (LVI present, PNI present, or high grade)

options.label: Wide local excision
options.recommendation: Consider if tumor < half of glans and patient accepts increased recurrence risk; close observation mandatory.

options.label: Partial penectomy
options.recommendation: Standard approach; intraoperative frozen sections recommended.

options.label: Total penectomy
options.recommendation: If partial penectomy not possible to achieve negative margins.

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: Brachytherapy or EBRT as above; category 2B for primary RT.

options.label: Chemo/RT
options.recommendation: EBRT with concurrent chemotherapy (category 3) for tumors <4 cm; total dose 65–70 Gy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: No routine adjuvant therapy; manage lymph nodes per PN-3/PN-4 based on nodal risk.

management_pathways.title: Primary Treatment for T2 or Greater

nodes.criteria: T2 (invasion into corpus spongiosum) or T3 (invasion into corpora cavernosum), N0

options.label: Partial penectomy
options.recommendation: Standard if functional stump can be preserved; negative margins with frozen sections.

options.label: Total penectomy
options.recommendation: If partial penectomy insufficient.

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: For tumors <4 cm: brachytherapy or EBRT 65–70 Gy; for tumors ≥4 cm: EBRT with concurrent chemo (category 3) 45–50.4 Gy to penile shaft/pelvic/inguinal nodes then boost to 65–70 Gy; or brachytherapy alone in select cases (category 2B).

options.label: Chemo/RT
options.recommendation: EBRT with concurrent chemotherapy as above.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Post-surgical RT to primary site if positive margins: 45–60 Gy if no gross disease; if gross disease remains, treat as T3-4 or N+.

nodes.criteria: T4 (invasion into adjacent structures: scrotum, prostate, pubic bone) or N+ surgically unresectable

options.label: Circumcision followed by EBRT with concurrent chemotherapy
options.recommendation: 45–50.4 Gy to whole penile shaft, pelvic nodes, bilateral inguinal nodes; boost primary and gross nodes to 60–70 Gy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Patients with initially unresectable T4 may be downstaged by response to chemotherapy and then undergo consolidative surgery.

management_pathways.title: Management of Non-Palpable Inguinal Lymph Nodes
branching_basis: Risk stratification based on primary lesion: low risk (PeIN, Ta, T1a) vs intermediate/high risk (T1b, any T2 or greater)

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: Clinical examination every 3–6 months per PN-8; no routine imaging. No further nodal intervention.

nodes.criteria: Intermediate/High risk (T1b, any T2 or greater)

options.label: Imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis
options.recommendation: CT, MRI, PET/CT, and/or chest x-ray with contrast as appropriate.

options.label: Bilateral inguinal lymph node dissection (ILND)
options.recommendation: Standard or modified/superficial inguinal dissection with intraoperative frozen section.

options.label: Bilateral dynamic sentinel node biopsy (DSNB)
options.recommendation: Only if treating physician has experience with this modality. If positive, proceed to ILND.

options.label: Prophylactic EBRT to inguinal lymph nodes
options.recommendation: For patients who are not surgical candidates or decline surgery.

adjuvant_logic.summary: If ILND or DSNB reveals positive nodes, manage per palpable node pathways: pN1 → surveillance; pN2–3 → consider PLND ± adjuvant RT/chemotherapy/chemoRT (category 2B).

management_pathways.title: Management of Palpable Non-Bulky Inguinal Lymph Nodes
branching_basis: Risk of primary lesion (low vs high)

nodes.criteria: Unilateral node(s) <4 cm mobile, low-risk primary lesion

options.label: Percutaneous lymph node biopsy
options.recommendation: Ultrasound- or CT-guided biopsy of most accessible node.

options.label: Excisional biopsy or Surveillance
options.recommendation: If negative on excisional biopsy, surveillance per PN-8.

options.label: Bilateral ILND
options.recommendation: Standard or modified ILND.

options.label: Neoadjuvant TIP followed by ILND
options.recommendation: Consider if high-risk features.

adjuvant_logic.summary: If pN1 after ILND: surveillance. If pN2–3: PLND ± adjuvant RT or chemotherapy or chemoRT (category 2B).

nodes.criteria: Unilateral node(s) <4 cm mobile, high-risk primary lesion

options.label: Bilateral ILND
options.recommendation: Proceed directly to ILND; consider neoadjuvant TIP.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Same as above: pN1 surveillance; pN2–3 consider PLND + adjuvant therapy.

nodes.criteria: Node(s) ≥4 cm (mobile or fixed) or unilateral node <4 cm fixed, or bilateral nodes

options.label: Manage as Palpable Bulky Inguinal Lymph Nodes (PN-5)
options.recommendation: See pathway for bulky nodes.

nodes.criteria: Enlarged pelvic lymph nodes on imaging

options.label: Manage as Enlarged Pelvic Lymph Nodes (PN-7)
options.recommendation: See pathway for pelvic nodes.

management_pathways.title: Management of Palpable Bulky Inguinal Lymph Nodes
branching_basis: Node size (≥4 cm mobile vs fixed)

options.label: Percutaneous lymph node biopsy
options.recommendation: Ultrasound- or CT-guided biopsy.

options.label: Neoadjuvant TIP followed by ILND (preferred)
options.recommendation: Four courses of TIP then consolidative ILND ± PLND.

options.label: ILND alone (if not eligible for TIP)
options.recommendation: Standard ILND; consider PLND.

options.label: RT or Chemo/RT
options.recommendation: Alternative if surgery not suitable.

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: Follow PN-8.

options.label: Same as positive biopsy pathway
options.recommendation: Neoadjuvant TIP, ILND, RT, or chemo/RT.

adjuvant_logic.summary: After ILND: if 0–1 positive nodes with viable disease → surveillance; if ≥2 positive nodes or extranodal extension → adjuvant chemotherapy (if not given preop) and/or if pelvic nodes positive → adjuvant RT or chemo/RT (category 2B).

nodes.criteria: Unilateral node(s) fixed or bilateral nodes (fixed or mobile)

options.label: Percutaneous lymph node biopsy (inguinal or pelvic)
options.recommendation: Biopsy most accessible node.

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: PN-8.

options.label: Neoadjuvant TIP chemotherapy (preferred) if response → ILND + PLND
options.recommendation: Surveillance if no viable disease; if response but extranodal extension → consider adjuvant RT or chemo/RT (category 2B).

options.label: If not eligible for TIP → ILND (preferred) or RT or chemo/RT
options.recommendation: Surgery or RT as appropriate.

options.label: No response/disease progression → Manage as Metastatic Disease (PN-10)
options.recommendation: Consider clinical trial, subsequent-line therapy, RT for local control, best supportive care.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Data suggest bilateral PLND should be performed if ≥4 positive inguinal nodes [141]. Postoperative RT or chemo/RT can be considered if extranodal extension (category 2B).

management_pathways.title: Management of Enlarged Pelvic Lymph Nodes

nodes.criteria: Pelvic lymph nodes enlarged on CT/MRI (not pathologic stage)

options.label: Percutaneous lymph node biopsy if technically feasible; if not, PET/CT to evaluate
options.recommendation: Biopsy most accessible inguinal or pelvic node.

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: Follow PN-8; manage according to inguinal node status.

options.label: Neoadjuvant TIP chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Then imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis.

options.label: Consolidation surgery (bilateral superficial/deep ILND + unilateral/bilateral PLND)
options.recommendation: Then surveillance; consider postoperative RT or chemo/RT if pN2–3 or extranodal extension (category 2B).

options.label: Metastatic disease management (PN-10)
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy, clinical trial, RT, best supportive care.

options.label: Chemo/RT
options.recommendation: Definitive chemoradiation.

management_pathways.title: Management of Recurrent Disease
branching_basis: Site of recurrence (local penile vs inguinal)

nodes.criteria: Local recurrence of penile lesion after initial penile sparing treatment

options.label: Treat according to recurrence stage
options.recommendation: Restage per PN-1 and PN-2; apply primary treatment pathways.

nodes.criteria: Inguinal recurrence, no prior inguinal lymphadenectomy or RT

options.label: Percutaneous lymph node biopsy
options.recommendation: If negative → surveillance (PN-8); if positive → ILND → if pN1 → surveillance; if pN2–3 → PLND ± adjuvant chemo or chemo/RT (category 2B) or chemotherapy alone (category 2B).

options.label: Percutaneous lymph node biopsy
options.recommendation: Then treat as for unilateral fixed/bilateral nodes (bulky pathway, PN-5).

nodes.criteria: Inguinal recurrence with prior inguinal lymphadenectomy or RT

options.label: Chemotherapy followed by ILND
options.recommendation: If resectable.

options.label: ILND alone
options.recommendation: If prior RT but no prior lymphadenectomy.

options.label: Chemo/RT (if no prior RT)
options.recommendation: If not previously irradiated.

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: After salvage treatment.

options.label: Imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis
options.recommendation: CT, MRI, PET/CT, and/or chest x-ray with contrast.

options.label: First-line systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Preferred: TIP (paclitaxel/ifosfamide/cisplatin). Other recommended: cisplatin/fluorouracil, cisplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab followed by pembrolizumab maintenance, or carboplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab followed by pembrolizumab maintenance. Not recommended: bleomycin-containing regimens [6].

options.label: Consolidation surgery (select patients)
options.recommendation: Bilateral superficial and deep ILND and unilateral/bilateral PLND.

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: PN-8.

options.label: Consider subsequent-line systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Preferred: clinical trial. Preferred for MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H: pembrolizumab. Useful in certain circumstances: paclitaxel, cetuximab. Any tumor-agnostic labels covering penile cancer based on MGPT.

options.label: RT for local control
options.recommendation: Palliative dose 30 Gy in 10 fractions.

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Per NCCN Palliative Care Guidelines.

items.text: Complete history and physical examination, including assessment of risk factors: balanitis, chronic inflammation, penile trauma, lack of neonatal circumcision, tobacco use, lichen sclerosus, poor hygiene, sexually transmitted disease (e.g., HIV) [4].

Document lesion characteristics: diameter, location, number of lesions, morphology (papillary, nodular, ulcerous, flat), relationship to other structures (submucosal, corpora spongiosa, cavernosa, urethra).
Assess performance status (ECOG) and social determinants of health using NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List [b].
Punch, excisional, or incisional biopsy of the penile lesion to confirm squamous cell carcinoma and determine grade, depth of invasion, LVI, PNI, and margin status.

Principles of Pathology: classify PeIN (high-grade HPV-associated or differentiated HPV-independent) and invasive SCC (HPV-associated subtypes: basaloid, warty, clear cell, lymphoepithelioma-like, mixed; HPV-independent subtypes: usual type, verrucous, papillary, sarcomatoid, mixed; NOS). Note aggressive histologies: basaloid, clear cell, sarcomatoid; verrucous has best prognosis and does not metastasize.
Assess HPV status (morphology, p16 immunohistochemistry, molecular methods) as it has prognostic significance (HPV/p16 positivity associated with better disease-specific survival) [15-17].
Assess HIV status, as co-infection increases risk of HPV and penile cancer [18,19,41].
Initial workup: imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis (CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis, chest x-ray or CT). For nodal evaluation if difficult to clinically assess: CT/MRI of pelvis with contrast.
Staging: same imaging; consider FDG-PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh) in patients with suspected inguinal lymph node-positive disease.
Treatment response assessment: imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis; consider FDG-PET/CT for suspected inguinal node-positive disease.
For metastatic disease, consider multigene panel testing (MGPT) in a CLIA-approved laboratory to identify rare actionable mutations and fusions.
For patients being considered for fluorouracil or capecitabine, consider DPYD testing per NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer.

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary curative modality for penile cancer, encompassing management of the primary tumor (organ-sparing, partial penectomy, total penectomy) and regional lymph nodes (inguinal and pelvic lymph node dissection). In select patients, surgery may be used as consolidation after systemic therapy or salvage for recurrence.
principles: Intraoperative frozen sections are recommended to determine negative margins for all penectomy procedures [PN-B].

Partial penectomy is standard for high-grade primary tumors when a functional penile stump can be preserved and negative margins obtained. Total penectomy is required if partial penectomy cannot achieve negative margins.
Invasion into the corpora cavernosum necessitates partial or total penectomy to achieve negative margins.

Standard or modified ILND or DSNB is indicated in patients without palpable inguinal adenopathy if high-risk features for nodal metastasis are present: lymphovascular invasion, ≥pT1b or ≥T2 any grade, >50% poorly differentiated [PN-B].
DSNB is only recommended if the treating physician has experience with this modality. If positive nodes found, proceed to ILND.

PLND should be considered at the time of or following ILND in patients with ≥2 positive ipsilateral inguinal nodes or extranodal extension. Bilateral PLND should be considered if ≥4 positive inguinal nodes total [141].

Consolidation surgery (bilateral superficial/deep ILND + unilateral/bilateral PLND) is recommended for select patients with stable or responding disease after neoadjuvant chemotherapy for metastatic disease.

procedures.name: Partial penectomy
procedures.approach: Standard surgical excision of the distal penis with negative margins; preservation of a functional penile stump.
procedures.indications: High-grade primary tumors when adequate stump can be preserved; invasion into corpora cavernosum; primary treatment for T1b or T2-3 tumors.
procedures.key_complications: Urethral meatal stenosis, penile stump recurrence, loss of penile length, erectile dysfunction.

procedures.name: Total penectomy
procedures.approach: Complete removal of the penis with perineal urethrostomy.
procedures.indications: When partial penectomy cannot achieve negative margins; extensive tumors involving the shaft or base.
procedures.key_complications: Perineal urethrostomy stenosis, loss of sexual function, psychological impact.

procedures.name: Inguinal lymph node dissection (ILND)
procedures.approach: Standard full-template ILND (Daseler's quadrilateral area: superior inguinal ligament, inferior fossa ovalis, lateral sartorius, medial adductor longus) or modified/superficial template. Robotic approach has been reported with lower morbidity in selected patients [126].
procedures.indications: Intermediate/high-risk primary tumors with nonpalpable nodes; positive DSNB; palpable nodes with positive biopsy; bulky inguinal nodes (preferred after neoadjuvant TIP).
procedures.key_complications: Wound infection, lymphocele, lymphedema, skin flap necrosis, deep vein thrombosis, nerve injury. Modified ILND reduces morbidity.

procedures.name: Pelvic lymph node dissection (PLND)
procedures.approach: Resection of external iliac, internal iliac, and obturator lymph nodes, unilateral or bilateral. Can be performed in same session or delayed after ILND.
procedures.indications: ≥2 positive ipsilateral inguinal nodes or extranodal extension; enlarged pelvic nodes on imaging; ≥4 positive inguinal nodes (bilateral PLND recommended) [141].
procedures.key_complications: Similar to ILND plus vascular injury, obturator nerve injury, ileus, pelvic lymphocele.

procedures.name: Dynamic sentinel node biopsy (DSNB)
procedures.approach: Lymphoscintigraphy with technetium-99m nanocolloid and patent blue dye; preoperative ultrasonography ± FNA; exploration of groins without visualized sentinel node. Serial sectioning and immunohistochemistry of specimen.
procedures.indications: Intermediate/high-risk nonpalpable nodes, performed at experienced centers (≥20 procedures/year) [107,120].
procedures.key_complications: False-negative rate ~5% after technique modifications [107,113]; wound infection, lymphedema (lower than ILND).

procedures.name: Glansectomy
procedures.approach: Removal of glans penis with frozen section of cavernosal bed and urethral stump; reconstruction with STSG or FTSG to create neoglans.
procedures.indications: Select patients with distal tumors (PeIN, Ta, T1a) on glans or prepuce when organ-sparing desired; category 2B for PeIN/Ta.
procedures.key_complications: Local recurrence (reported 2.6%–16.7%), meatal stenosis, graft loss, reduced penile sensation, erectile dysfunction (though normal erections reported in 50%–100% of cases) [60].

procedures.name: Mohs micrographic surgery
procedures.approach: Serial excision of thin layers of cancerous skin with immediate microscopic examination until negative margins.
procedures.indications: Alternative to wide local excision in select cases, especially small superficial lesions on proximal shaft to avoid total penectomy [2,66]. Success rate declines with higher stage disease.
procedures.key_complications: Local recurrence, higher with advanced T stage; need for multiple excisions; bleeding, infection.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resection is considered appropriate when negative margins can be achieved with acceptable functional outcomes. For primary tumors, invasion into corpora cavernosum requires partial/total penectomy. For nodal disease, resectability is determined by absence of fixation to underlying structures (e.g., femoral vessels, pubic bone) and absence of distant metastases. Patients with bulky, fixed inguinal nodes or pelvic nodes should first receive neoadjuvant chemotherapy; consolidation surgery is reserved for those with stable or responding disease. If unresectable, definitive CRT or palliative approaches are indicated.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiotherapy is used as primary treatment for penile preservation (T1-2 N0, tumors <4 cm preferred), adjuvant therapy after lymph node dissection (for high-risk features, positive pelvic nodes), and palliative treatment for metastatic disease. Evidence is limited; recommendations are category 2B (primary RT) or category 3 (chemo/RT) due to nonuniform consensus.
principles: Circumcision should always precede RT to prevent radiation-related complications.

For primary RT, brachytherapy (interstitial implant) is preferred over EBRT for small (<4 cm) tumors confined to glans [PN-C]. EBRT uses conventional fractionation with appropriate bolus to primary lesion with 2 cm margins.
Adjuvant EBRT to inguinal and pelvic nodes: 45–50.4 Gy; boost gross nodes and areas of extracapsular extension to 65–70 Gy.
Palliative RT: consider 30 Gy in 10 fractions.
Prophylactic EBRT to inguinal nodes (category 2B) is an option for patients who are not surgical candidates or decline surgery.

dose_frameworks.name: Primary brachytherapy (T1-2 N0, tumor <4 cm)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 65–70 Gy (for EBRT equivalent); brachytherapy dose not specified in source but typical 60 Gy over 5-7 days
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: Not specified for brachytherapy; EBRT 1.8–2.0 Gy/fraction
dose_frameworks.fractions: Not specified for brachytherapy; EBRT 33–35 fractions
dose_frameworks.indication: T1-2 N0, tumor <4 cm, after circumcision (category 2B)

dose_frameworks.name: Primary EBRT + concurrent chemo (T1-2, tumor ≥4 cm or T3-4 N+)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 45–50.4 Gy to whole penile shaft/pelvic/inguinal nodes, then boost primary + gross nodes to 60–70 Gy
dose_frameworks.indication: T3-4 or N+ surgically unresectable; T1-2 with tumor ≥4 cm (category 3 for chemo/RT)

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant EBRT (post-ILND for high-risk node-positive)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 45–50.4 Gy to inguinal and pelvic lymph nodes; boost to 65–70 Gy for gross nodes/extracapsular extension
dose_frameworks.indication: pN2–3 disease, positive pelvic nodes, extranodal extension (category 2B for non-bulky; recommended for bulky and enlarged pelvic nodes)

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT
dose_frameworks.indication: For local control of unresectable inguinal or bone metastases, after chemotherapy or for symptomatic relief

dose_frameworks.name: Prophylactic EBRT to inguinal nodes
dose_frameworks.indication: Patients with intermediate/high-risk primary who are not surgical candidates or decline surgery (category 2B)

approaches.name: Brachytherapy alone (T1-2 N0, <4 cm)
approaches.dose_fractionation: Interstitial implant; typical dose not explicitly stated in source
approaches.indication: Category 2B; after circumcision; highest evidence for glans-confined tumors (10-year cause-specific survival 92% in one series [79]; 84% in another [77])
approaches.key_trial: de Crevoisier et al [79]: 144 patients with glans-restricted penile cancer, 10-year CSS 92%; larger tumors >4 cm had higher recurrence risk. Crook et al [77]: 67 patients T1-2 (select T3), 10-year CSS 84%.
approaches.toxicities: Urethral stenosis, telangiectasia, necrosis, penile pain, erectile dysfunction; risk increases with tumor size >4 cm.

approaches.name: EBRT alone (T1-2 N0, <4 cm)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 65–70 Gy total, conventional fractionation, with bolus and 2 cm margins
approaches.key_trial: Limited data; outcomes from retrospective series.
approaches.toxicities: Acute: skin desquamation, urethritis, cystitis. Late: fibrosis, urethral stricture, telangiectasia, penile necrosis.

approaches.name: EBRT with concurrent chemotherapy (primary, T1-2 N0 ≥4 cm or T3-4 N+)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45–50.4 Gy to whole penile shaft, pelvic, and bilateral inguinal nodes; boost primary and gross nodes to 60–70 Gy
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Preferred radiosensitizing agents: cisplatin ± fluorouracil, fluorouracil/mitomycin. Other recommended: capecitabine [PN-D3].
approaches.indication: Category 3 for primary treatment; greater acceptance for unresectable T3-4 or N+ disease.
approaches.key_trial: No randomized trials in penile cancer; data extrapolated from anal/vulvar cancer [152-155]. Anecdotal reports with mixed results [156-159].
approaches.toxicities: Similar to EBRT plus systemic toxicity of chemotherapy (nephrotoxicity, myelosuppression, mucositis). Higher rates of acute skin and mucosal reactions.

approaches.name: Adjuvant EBRT or Chemo/RT (post-ILND/PLND)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45–50.4 Gy to inguinal/pelvic nodes; boost to 65–70 Gy for gross residual or extracapsular extension
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Cisplatin ± fluorouracil, fluorouracil/mitomycin, or capecitabine if chemo/RT chosen.
approaches.indication: Recommended for palpable bulky inguinal nodes or enlarged pelvic nodes; consider for pN2–3 disease (category 2B) or local recurrence (category 2B).
approaches.key_trial: Retrospective multicenter: adjuvant pelvic RT improved DSS (14.4 vs 8 months, P=.023) [149]; adjuvant RT for stage III penile cancer improved OS (HR 0.58 [0.39–0.86]) [150]. Systematic review by EAU showed no significant benefit [151].
approaches.toxicities: Lymphedema, wound healing complications, enteritis, cystitis, bone marrow suppression.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for penile cancer is used in neoadjuvant, adjuvant, first-line metastatic, and subsequent-line settings. The backbone is platinum-based chemotherapy, with TIP (paclitaxel, ifosfamide, cisplatin) as the preferred regimen across neoadjuvant, adjuvant, and first-line metastatic settings. Cisplatin/fluorouracil is an alternative. The addition of pembrolizumab to platinum-based chemotherapy followed by pembrolizumab maintenance is a newer option for metastatic disease based on the HERCULES trial. There is no standard subsequent-line therapy; clinical trials are preferred. Tumor-agnostic indications (MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H) may apply. Bleomycin-containing regimens are associated with unacceptable toxicity and are not recommended [6].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Phase II trial in N2-3 M0 penile cancer: ORR 50%, estimated long-term PFS 36.7%. Improved PFS and OS associated with objective response [1]. Preferred for patients with ≥4 cm inguinal nodes (fixed or mobile) with positive FNA [2]. Patients not eligible for TIP should undergo surgery without neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: None specified; cisplatin/fluorouracil is not recommended for neoadjuvant setting [PN-D1].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: No sufficient definitive data; by extrapolation from neoadjuvant data, 4 courses of TIP reasonable if not given preoperatively and pathology shows high-risk features (pelvic node metastases, extranodal extension, bilateral inguinal nodes, ≥4 cm tumor in nodes).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cisplatin/fluorouracil [3,4] (other recommended). Adjuvant EBRT or chemo/RT also considered for high-risk features.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Reasonable for palliative treatment including distant metastases [1]. Alternative: cisplatin/fluorouracil ± pembrolizumab followed by pembrolizumab maintenance [5]; or carboplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab followed by pembrolizumab maintenance [5].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cisplatin/fluorouracil alone [3,4]; cisplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab maintenance [5]; carboplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab maintenance [5]. Bleomycin-containing regimens not recommended [6].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Clinical trial; Pembrolizumab (if MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H, tumor-agnostic labels) [7-10]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: No standard subsequent-line therapy; evidence limited [13]. KEYNOTE-158: ORR 34.3% in MSI-H/dMMR non-colorectal cancer, median PFS 4.1 mo, OS 23.5 mo [179]. TMB-H group ORR 29% vs 6% [181].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Paclitaxel [11], cetuximab [12] (useful in certain circumstances, especially if not previously treated with similar class). Any tumor-agnostic labels covering penile cancer based on multigene panel testing results.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Extrapolated from other SCC sites (anal, head/neck).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine [17,18] (other recommended). DPYD testing recommended for fluorouracil or capecitabine.

key_regimens.name: TIP
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity (paclitaxel, cisplatin), hemorrhagic cystitis (ifosfamide; requires mesna), fatigue, alopecia, nausea/vomiting.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Fluorouracil
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, mucositis, hand-foot syndrome, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, neuropathy, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea. DPYD testing recommended.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Fluorouracil + Pembrolizumab (followed by Pembrolizumab maintenance)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks for 6 cycles, then maintenance Pembrolizumab 200 mg IV Day 1 every 3 weeks for up to 34 cycles
key_regimens.key_toxicities: As above for cisplatin/fluorouracil, plus immune-related adverse events (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, dermatitis, nephritis, etc.). Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab (different dosing).

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Fluorouracil + Pembrolizumab (followed by Pembrolizumab maintenance)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks for 6 cycles, then maintenance Pembrolizumab 200 mg IV Day 1 every 3 weeks for up to 34 cycles
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Same as cisplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab but with less nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity; more myelosuppression from carboplatin.

key_regimens.name: Paclitaxel monotherapy
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Peripheral neuropathy, myelosuppression, alopecia, hypersensitivity reactions.

key_regimens.name: Cetuximab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Acneiform rash, hypomagnesemia, infusion reactions, diarrhea.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment should be performed after neoadjuvant systemic therapy (typically after 4 cycles of TIP) using imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis (CT, MRI, PET/CT, and/or chest x-ray). For patients on first-line metastatic therapy, reassess after 2-3 cycles. For patients undergoing consolidation surgery, restaging is done post-chemotherapy to determine resectability. For surveillance, schedule is based on initial treatment and nodal status per PN-8.
response_logic: Complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) or stable disease (SD) after neoadjuvant therapy: proceed with consolidation surgery (bilateral superficial/deep ILND and unilateral/bilateral PLND) in select patients.

No response or disease progression after neoadjuvant or first-line systemic therapy: consider subsequent-line systemic therapy, clinical trial, RT for local control, and/or best supportive care per NCCN Palliative Care Guidelines.

After ILND/PLND: if viable tumor present in surgical specimen, assess for adjuvant therapy (chemotherapy, RT, or chemo/RT) based on pathological features (pN2–3, extranodal extension, positive margins).
For primary RT: response assessed by clinical examination and imaging; suspicious residual or recurrent disease should be biopsied and managed per recurrence pathways.
imaging_recommendations: Treatment response assessment: imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis (CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis, chest x-ray or CT). Consider FDG-PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh) for suspected inguinal lymph node-positive disease [PN-E].
Surveillance for pN0/N1: clinical examination every 6 months for years 1–2, then annually years 3–4; CT abdomen/pelvis and chest radiograph at same intervals.
Surveillance for pN2/N3: clinical examination every 3 months year 1, then every 6 months years 2–4; CT abdomen/pelvis and chest CT at same intervals.
If abnormal clinical examination, obesity, or prior inguinal surgery, consider ultrasound, CT with contrast, or MRI of inguinal region.
For patients on active surveillance of clinically negative nodes (low risk): clinical examination of penis and inguinal region per schedule.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Persistent or suspicious inguinal lymph node after treatment: percutaneous biopsy (ultrasound- or CT-guided) of most accessible node. If negative, consider excisional biopsy.
Biopsy-proven residual disease after neoadjuvant therapy: proceed with surgical resection (ILND/PLND) if resectable. If unresectable, consider RT or chemo/RT.
Local recurrence of primary after penile-sparing treatment: treat according to recurrence stage per primary treatment pathways (PN-1, PN-2).
Inguinal recurrence with prior lymphadenectomy or RT: consider chemotherapy followed by ILND (if resectable), ILND alone, or chemo/RT (if no prior RT).
For metastatic disease progression: consider subsequent-line systemic therapy, clinical trial, RT for palliation, best supportive care.

prognosis.overall: Penile squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is a rare disease with a 5-year survival rate of approximately 65% overall. For patients with localized disease, the 5-year survival rate is 79%; for regional disease, 57%; and for distant metastatic disease, 10% [5], [6]. The median age at diagnosis in the United States is 68 years, and earlier diagnosis is associated with improved outcomes [9]. HPV or p16 positivity is reported as a favorable prognostic factor, associated with better disease-specific survival (DSS) [15], [16], [17]. Visceral metastases and an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance score ≥1 are poor prognostic factors for both overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS) [27]. The presence and extent of inguinal lymph node metastases is the single most important prognostic indicator [32]. Pathologic prognostic factors include histologic subtype (e.g., verrucous carcinoma has best prognosis; basaloid, clear cell, sarcomatoid are aggressive), histologic grade (3-tiered), depth of invasion/tumor thickness, perineural invasion, lymphovascular space invasion, resection margin status, and pathologic stage [PN-F].

by_stage.stage: Localized (confined to penis, no nodal/distant metastases)
by_stage.context: Based on American Cancer Society data from the NCCN guideline overview [5], [6]. Includes Stage 0is, 0a, I, IIA, IIB per AJCC 8th edition.

by_stage.stage: Regional (inguinal or pelvic lymph node involvement, no distant metastases)
by_stage.context: Includes stage IIIA, IIIB, and some stage IV (T4 any N M0, or any T N3 M0) [5], [6].

prognostic_factors: Visceral metastases and ECOG PS ≥1 - poor OS/PFS [27]
Inguinal lymph node involvement (number, site, extracapsular extension) - strongest prognostic factor [6], [32]
Histologic subtype: verrucous (best prognosis), basaloid/clear cell/sarcomatoid (aggressive) [PN-F]
Histologic grade (3-tiered): grade 3 poorly differentiated/undifferentiated is high risk [29], [38]
Perineural invasion and lymphovascular space invasion
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis in penile cancer is heavily dependent on nodal status. Patients with nonpalpable nodes and low-risk primary lesions have a very low risk of occult metastasis (<17%) [99], [135]. Conversely, patients with ≥2 positive inguinal nodes or extranodal extension have a significantly worse prognosis and are candidates for adjuvant therapy [147], [150]. The 5-year survival for patients with pelvic lymph node metastases is approximately 10% [6], [165], [166], [167], [168], [169].

disease_related.management: ILND with or without neoadjuvant TIP chemotherapy; consider PLND for ≥2 positive inguinal nodes or extranodal extension [PN-4], [PN-5], [PN-6].
Neoadjuvant TIP followed by consolidation surgery (bilateral ILND + PLND) or chemoradiotherapy for nonsurgical candidates [PN-7], [PN-D].
Systemic chemotherapy (TIP, cisplatin/fluorouracil ± pembrolizumab), clinical trial, RT for local control, best supportive care [PN-10], [PN-D].
Supportive care; consider physical therapy, compression garments. Specific management per NCCN Survivorship guidelines [PN-8 footnote x].

chemotherapy: TIP (paclitaxel/ifosfamide/cisplatin): myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, fatigue [PN-D].
Cisplatin/fluorouracil: nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, mucositis, hand-foot syndrome, cardiotoxicity; significant toxicity may require dose reductions [3], [4].
Cisplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab: immune-related adverse events (irAEs) including pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies; manageable safety profile [5], [173].
Carboplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab: similar toxicity profile with carboplatin instead of cisplatin.
Bleomycin-containing regimens: unacceptable pulmonary toxicity; not recommended [6].
immunotherapy: Pembrolizumab (monotherapy for MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H tumors): irAEs as above; treatment-related fatalities reported (pneumonia) [179], [181].
surgery: Penectomy (partial or total): loss of penile length, altered urination, sexual dysfunction, phantom sensations [PN-B].

Penile organ-sparing approaches (topical therapy, laser, wide local excision, glansectomy, Mohs): local recurrence risk higher (28% vs 5% for penectomy); skin graft complications (necrosis, contracture), urethral stenosis [44], [136].
ILND/PLND: wound infection, seroma, lymphedema, nerve injury, skin necrosis, venous thromboembolism; modified template reduces morbidity [127], [128].
DSNB: false-negative rate up to 5% with experienced hands; groin exploration may be needed [107], [113], [114].
radiation: EBRT: acute skin reactions, moist desquamation, fibrosis, urethral stricture, penile atrophy, impotence; higher risk of complications when combined with chemotherapy [PN-C].
Brachytherapy: radiation necrosis, soft tissue ulcer, urethral stenosis; risk of recurrence higher for tumors >4 cm [79], [80].
Palliative RT: short-course 30 Gy/10 fractions; may cause local toxicity but generally well-tolerated [PN-C].

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is an integral part of penile cancer management. The NCCN guidelines emphasize use of the Distress Thermometer and Problem List to address social determinants of health [PN-1 footnote b]. Best supportive care is recommended for patients with no response or disease progression after systemic therapy, in accordance with NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care [PN-10]. Survivorship care follows the NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship [PN-8 footnote x].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Not specifically addressed in the penile cancer guideline; standard oncology nutrition support per institutional practice.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not detailed; for platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin, carboplatin), standard antiemetic prophylaxis (NK1 antagonist + 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone) is recommended per NCCN Antiemesis Guidelines.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Not specifically mentioned; for neutropenic risk with TIP or cisplatin/fluorouracil, primary prophylaxis with G-CSF may be considered per NCCN Myeloid Growth Factors Guidelines.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not specifically addressed; patients undergoing major surgery (ILND, PLND, penectomy) should receive VTE prophylaxis per institutional protocols and NCCN VTE Guidelines.
supportive_care.pain_management: For palliative RT or advanced disease, consider RT for local control (e.g., 30 Gy/10 fractions) and best supportive care per NCCN Palliative Care Guidelines [PN-C], [PN-10].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List to identify distress; referrals to psychosocial services, support groups, or sexual health counseling as needed [PN-1 footnote b].
supportive_care.dental_care: Not addressed. For patients receiving RT to the penile area, standard dental evaluation prior to RT is prudent, but no specific recommendations given.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Follow-up schedule is stratified by initial treatment of primary lesion and nodal status. For patients treated with topical/local therapy: clinical examination of penis and inguinal region every 3 months for years 1–2, then every 6 months for years 3–5, then every 12 months for years 5–10 [PN-8]. For partial/radical penectomy: clinical examination every 6 months for years 1–2, then every 12 months for years 3–10 [PN-8]. For lymph node management: pN0/N1 patients: clinical examination every 6 months for years 1–2, then every 12 months for years 3–4; with CT abdomen/pelvis and chest radiograph. pN2/N3 patients: clinical examination every 6 months for years 1–2, then every 12 months for years 3–4; with CT abdomen/pelvis and chest CT: year 1 every 3 months, years 2–4 every 6 months [PN-8]. If abnormal clinical examination, obesity, or prior inguinal surgery, consider ultrasound, CT with contrast, or MRI of inguinal region [PN-8 footnote y,z].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: A large retrospective review of 700 patients found that 92% of all recurrences were detected within 5 years of primary treatment [136]. Penile-sparing therapies carry a significantly higher risk of local recurrence (28%) than partial/total penectomy (5%) and thus require closer surveillance [136]. Nodal recurrences tend to occur earlier: median time to recurrence 10 months for distant, 12 months for inguinal, 10.5 months for pelvic, and 44.5 months for local (with >95% of distant/inguinal/pelvic recurrences within 48 months, while it took 127 months for 95% of local recurrences) [160].
late_effects_screening: Not explicitly detailed in the penile cancer guideline; refer to NCCN Survivorship Guidelines for general late effects screening after cancer treatment [PN-8 footnote x].
Late effects from RT: penile fibrosis, urethral stricture, erectile dysfunction, secondary malignancies (rare).
Late effects from chemotherapy: peripheral neuropathy (taxanes), nephrotoxicity (cisplatin), ototoxicity.
Lymphedema screening and management after ILND/PLND.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Local recurrence after penile-sparing treatment: 28% vs 5% after penectomy [136]. Nodal recurrence: N0 patients 2%, node-positive 19% [136]. Inguinal recurrence after ILND: median time 12 months; pelvic recurrence 10.5 months; distant recurrence 10 months [160]. Most recurrences occur within 5 years, but late local recurrences can occur up to 127 months [160]. Management of recurrence is guided by extent: local recurrence after penile-sparing is re-staged; isolated inguinal recurrence may be treated with salvage surgery/chemoradiation [PN-9].

clinical_follow_up_schedule.scenario: Primary lesion treated with topical or local therapy
clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: Clinical examination of penis and inguinal region: years 1–2 every 3 months; years 3–5 every 6 months; years 5–10 every 12 months [PN-8].

clinical_follow_up_schedule.scenario: Primary lesion treated with partial or radical penectomy
clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: Clinical examination of penis and inguinal region: years 1–2 every 6 months; years 3–10 every 12 months [PN-8].
Clinical examination: years 1–2 every 6 months; years 3–4 every 12 months. Imaging: CT abdomen/pelvis and chest radiograph at each visit [PN-8].
Clinical examination: years 1–2 every 6 months; years 3–4 every 12 months. Imaging: CT abdomen/pelvis and chest CT: year 1 every 3 months; years 2–4 every 6 months [PN-8].

imaging_strategy: Initial workup/staging: imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis (CT or MRI) with contrast when appropriate [PN-E]. For pelvic nodal evaluation, CT/MRI of pelvis. Consider FDG-PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh) for suspected inguinal lymph node-positive disease [PN-E].
Treatment response assessment: imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis as above; consider FDG-PET/CT for response/progression in node-positive patients [PN-E].

Surveillance: For pN0/N1: CT abdomen/pelvis + chest radiograph every 6–12 months. For pN2/N3: CT abdomen/pelvis + chest CT more frequently (every 3 months year 1, every 6 months years 2–4). If abnormal clinical exam, obesity, or prior inguinal surgery: consider ultrasound, CT, or MRI of groin [PN-8], [PN-E].
laboratory_monitoring: No specific laboratory surveillance recommended in the guideline. For patients on chemotherapy, standard complete blood counts and renal/hepatic function monitoring per drug-specific protocols. For pembrolizumab, monitor thyroid function and other immune-related labs as indicated.
supportive_follow_up: Survivorship care per NCCN Survivorship Guidelines [PN-8 footnote x].
Distress screening using NCCN Distress Thermometer [PN-1 footnote b].
Consider referral to sexual health specialist for erectile dysfunction or body image issues.

special_situations.key_message: By definition a well-differentiated tumor; does not metastasize. Only surveillance of inguinal lymph nodes required [PN-3 footnote h], [PN-F].
special_situations.management: Penile-sparing approaches (wide local excision, circumcision) are appropriate; no need for ILND unless invasive features develop.
special_situations.evidence: NCCN Panel consensus; verrucous carcinoma is classified as low malignant potential [30], [PN-F].

special_situations.key_message: HPV infection is a risk factor, and HIV increases risk of HPV acquisition and decreases clearance [18]. Oral HPV common in HIV-positive males [19].
special_situations.management: Assess HIV and HPV status during evaluation [PN-1]. Standard treatment should be offered; consider multidisciplinary care including infectious disease specialist. No specific alterations to cancer management are recommended in the guideline.
special_situations.evidence: The guideline notes increased risk and prognostic significance [14], [15], [17], [18], [19].

special_situations.situation: Patients with psoriasis on PUVA therapy
special_situations.key_message: PUVA treatment increases penile cancer incidence 286 times compared to general population [26].
special_situations.management: Shield genital area during PUVA therapy; any penile lesion should be closely monitored. Standard management once diagnosed.

special_situations.key_message: 2% to 9% risk of developing penile carcinoma [23], [24], [25].
special_situations.management: Surveillance and early biopsy of suspicious lesions.

special_situations.key_message: May be downstaged by chemotherapy to allow curative resection [PN-D 1 OF 5].
special_situations.management: Neoadjuvant TIP chemotherapy; if response, consider consolidative surgery (bilateral ILND + PLND). If no response, proceed to metastatic disease management.
special_situations.evidence: Phase II data from Pagliaro et al [1].

special_situations.situation: Patients not eligible for cisplatin (e.g., renal impairment, hearing loss, neuropathy)
special_situations.key_message: Carboplatin-based regimens may be considered; TIP is preferred for neoadjuvant but if not eligible, proceed directly to ILND [PN-D].
special_situations.management: Carboplatin/fluorouracil + pembrolizumab is an option for first-line metastatic disease [PN-D 2]. Alternatively, cetuximab or paclitaxel as later lines.
special_situations.evidence: NCCN Panel recommendation based on expert consensus and available data.

special_situations.situation: Patients with MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H tumors
special_situations.key_message: Pembrolizumab has tumor-agnostic indications for these biomarkers after prior progression and no satisfactory alternatives [7], [8], [9], [10].
special_situations.management: Pembrolizumab monotherapy preferred for subsequent-line treatment if tumor meets criteria. Consider MGPT for all metastatic patients [PN-10 footnote bb].

clinical_pearls: Penile cancer is rare but aggressive; early diagnosis is critical. Any suspicious penile lesion warrants biopsy (punch, excisional, or incisional) with assessment of HPV and HIV status [PN-1].

Inguinal lymph node status is the strongest prognostic factor. For intermediate/high-risk primary tumors (T1b or ≥T2), bilateral ILND or DSNB is recommended even if nodes are nonpalpable, because occult metastases occur in up to 68–73% [64], [99], [135].

Neoadjuvant TIP chemotherapy prior to ILND is preferred for bulky inguinal nodes (≥4 cm fixed) or enlarged pelvic nodes. The phase II study showed 50% response rate and 36.7% estimated long-term progression-free survival [1].

Penile-sparing approaches (topical therapy, laser, wide local excision) are appropriate for PeIN, Ta, and pT1a lesions but require close surveillance due to higher local recurrence (28% vs 5% for penectomy) [136].

Radiation therapy as primary treatment is controversial; brachytherapy with interstitial implant (category 2B) or EBRT (category 2B) may be considered for T1–2 N0 tumors <4 cm. Chemoradiotherapy is category 3 for primary treatment due to limited data [PN-C].
